memory.summary: The smell is driving me crazy and I can't focus, but asking my roommate to clean feels like too much drama right now. I'll just grab my stuff and head to the library instead.
The library saves my focus, but ignoring the smell means I'll still be exhausted tonight. Why stress about fixing it when I can just avoid the room?
I finally got some reading done, but I'm still too tired to care about the smell or my long-term progress. As long as I keep moving, everything else can wait.
I pushed through the reading despite feeling drained, but I'm still too tired to care about the smell or my long-term progress. As long as I keep moving, everything else can wait.
I barely moved the needle on that reading, but I'm too exhausted to worry about the smell or consequences. As long as I'm not fighting anyone, I'll just keep coasting.
I barely moved the needle on that reading again, but I'm too drained to care about the long-term consequences. As long as I'm not fighting anyone, I'll just coast until mealtime.
I'm back in my room and too drained to think about anything but resting. As long as I avoid trouble, the rest can wait until later.
I finally spoke up about the smell, and roommate_R actually listened without getting mad. It feels good to keep things smooth without any drama.
I finally got roommate_R to listen without a fight, which feels great. Now that the drama is gone, I can just coast until dinner.
I made it to the dining hall, finally. Being here with my friends is worth more than worrying about that unfinished assignment later.
Roommate_R is back, but I'm too drained to start a new conversation. Better to just let it slide and avoid any friction right now.
I let the moment pass again; nothing changes, but at least I avoided a fight. My friends are more important than finishing this assignment tonight.
